headline: Sarah’s clarity, influence, and stakeholder impact are the problem—she waits for analysis to finish, so decisions get shaped late.
summary_bullets: Sarah owns solid technical output, but repeatedly frames the gap as “influence is uneven” and “shaping decisions early” not happening consistently.
She identifies a specific mechanism: “wait until analysis is complete before socializing direction,” which she links to late surprise and trust erosion.
Her commitments are process-focused but still vague on leverage: “define what good looks like” and “lead one cross-team decision” without naming the stakeholder, decision, or escalation path.
understanding_paragraph: Sarah understands the performance bar in terms of influence and early decision-shaping, not just technical delivery. The new detail is her causal story: she believes late socialization “slows trust because stakeholders feel surprised late.” What’s still missing is a concrete plan for who she will influence, when she will surface tradeoffs, and how she will escalate cross-functional friction before analysis completes.

axes.meaning: Weak-to-moderate wellbeing risk signal: stress or fatigue is likely affecting pace and recovery, worth watching over the next few weeks.
Leaning negative on ownership of influence: she delivers, but investment in stakeholder leverage is inconsistent.
A real clarity gap: priorities and expectations around early decision-shaping and stakeholder narrative are not landing cleanly.
Very weak growth signal: she names what to improve, but the plan lacks measurable stretch and feedback mechanics.

brutal_truth_employee: Sarah says, “I tend to wait until analysis is complete before socializing direction,” and she connects it to impact: “That slows trust because stakeholders feel surprised late.” Her stated fix is “a tighter narrative on tradeoffs,” but her commitment doesn’t yet name the decision, stakeholders, or timing to prevent late surprise.
brutal_truth_manager: When Sarah gave the causal mechanism—“wait until analysis is complete before socializing direction”—the conversation should have forced a concrete stakeholder-and-timeline commitment. Next time, don’t accept “define what good looks like” without requiring the exact decision she’ll shape, the first draft/tradeoff she’ll share, and the escalation trigger if partners disagree.

next_actions.action: Schedule a 30-minute follow-up with Sarah to pick one named cross-team decision and fill: stakeholder list, first tradeoff message date, and what she will ask them to decide.
Ask Sarah to draft a one-page “early direction” update template and share it with you for approval before she sends it to non-technical partners.
Start the 1:1 with: “What decision did you shape early, what did you share before analysis was complete, and what changed because of it?”

watch_for: Before next 1:1: if Sarah cannot name a specific stakeholder decision she influenced before analysis completion, treat the influence gap as unchanged.
Within two weeks: if “tighter narrative on tradeoffs” never turns into an early written update sent to non-technical partners, escalation readiness is still missing.
